meta.name: io_uring High-Performance Server
meta.description: Build a high-performance TCP server using Linux io_uring, progressing from basic SQ/CQ operations through advanced features including zero-copy I/O, provided buffer rings, SQ polling, and linked operations. Benchmark against epoll.
meta.essence: Asynchronous I/O through shared ring buffers between kernel and userspace, eliminating syscall overhead by batching submission/completion queue operations, using provided buffer rings for scalable memory management, and enabling zero-copy data transfer for high-throughput workloads.
meta.why_important: Building this teaches you modern Linux I/O architecture that outperforms traditional epoll/select mechanisms, a critical skill for high-performance systems like databases, web servers, and real-time applications where I/O latency directly impacts throughput.
learning_outcomes: Implement submission and completion queue management with memory-mapped ring buffers
Design buffer management strategies including fixed buffers and provided buffer rings
Build batched syscall interfaces that minimize kernel transitions
Handle short reads/writes and partial I/O completions correctly
Debug asynchronous I/O race conditions and completion ordering issues
Optimize network servers using linked operations, SQ polling, and registered file descriptors
Benchmark I/O subsystems and analyze performance differences between io_uring and epoll
Implement proper resource cleanup and error handling for kernel ring buffers

prerequisites.name: Linux system calls (read, write, mmap, syscall ABI)

milestones.name: Basic SQ/CQ Operations & Buffer Strategies
milestones.description: Set up io_uring instance, perform basic submission/completion queue operations with correct memory barriers, and implement both fixed buffer registration and provided buffer rings (IORING_REGISTER_PBUF_RING).
concepts: Memory barriers for ring head/tail synchronization
acceptance_criteria: io_uring instance is initialized with io_uring_setup syscall and SQ/CQ rings are mmap'd into userspace
SQ and CQ sizes are configured correctly — CQ is at least 2x SQ size (kernel default) to prevent CQ overflow
Memory barriers (io_uring_smp_store_release for tail, io_uring_smp_load_acquire for head) are correctly placed between ring updates
Batched submission demonstrated by submitting 10+ SQEs with a single io_uring_enter call and verifying all CQEs are harvested
Fixed buffers are registered with IORING_REGISTER_BUFFERS and used with IORING_OP_READ_FIXED/WRITE_FIXED; benchmark shows reduced per-op overhead vs non-fixed
Provided buffer ring is set up with IORING_REGISTER_PBUF_RING; operations select buffers from the ring and the buffer group ID is correctly specified in SQEs
CQE error handling checks res field for negative errno values and handles EAGAIN, ECANCELED, and EBUSY appropriately
All registered buffers and rings are properly unregistered during cleanup
pitfalls: SQ and CQ have separate sizes; CQ overflow (IORING_CQ_OVERFLOW) silently drops completions if CQ is full and you don't check the overflow counter
Missing memory barriers between head/tail updates causes data races even on x86 due to compiler reordering
io_uring_enter with IORING_ENTER_GETEVENTS blocks; without it, you must poll CQ manually
Provided buffer rings require kernel 5.19+; check kernel version or feature probe with IORING_REGISTER_PBUF_RING
Fixed buffer registration fails if buffers are not page-aligned on some kernel versions
deliverables: io_uring setup module with mmap'd shared memory rings and configurable queue depths
SQE preparation helpers for read/write with both regular and fixed buffer variants
CQE harvesting loop with proper error code checking and overflow detection
Provided buffer ring setup and replenishment logic for scalable buffer management
Batched submission test demonstrating multiple ops per io_uring_enter call

milestones.name: File I/O Server with Registered Resources
milestones.description: Build a file server using io_uring for all I/O operations, utilizing registered file descriptors and fixed buffers, with correct handling of short reads.
concepts: Short reads and partial I/O completions
Direct I/O vs buffered I/O tradeoffs
acceptance_criteria: File server serves read requests using IORING_OP_READ with correct file descriptor and offset parameters
File descriptors are registered with IORING_REGISTER_FILES and SQEs use IOSQE_FIXED_FILE flag with the registered index
Short reads (CQE res < requested bytes) are handled by resubmitting a read for the remaining bytes at the updated offset
Multiple concurrent file reads (at least 64 in-flight) are handled with correct buffer-to-request association
Direct I/O (O_DIRECT) variant is implemented with properly aligned buffers (512-byte or 4K-byte alignment depending on filesystem)
Benchmark demonstrates at least 2x throughput improvement over synchronous pread for 64+ concurrent 4KB random reads on SSD
Buffer lifetime is guaranteed to extend until CQE is harvested; no buffer is reused while an async operation references it
pitfalls: IORING_OP_READ requires explicit offset; passing -1 uses the file's current position which is racy with concurrent ops
Registered file descriptors use indices (not fd numbers); confusing them causes EBADF
O_DIRECT requires buffer alignment to filesystem block size; misalignment causes EINVAL
Buffer reuse before CQE harvest causes data corruption that is extremely difficult to debug
Short reads are common on pipes, sockets, and even files near EOF; treating them as errors breaks the server
deliverables: File server handling concurrent async reads using IORING_OP_READ
Registered file descriptor table with add/remove/update support
Short read handler resubmitting partial completions
Buffer pool with ownership tracking preventing premature reuse
Benchmark suite comparing io_uring vs synchronous and vs O_DIRECT file I/O

milestones.name: TCP Network Server
milestones.description: Build a TCP echo server using io_uring for accept, read, write, and close operations. Handle connection lifecycle entirely through io_uring with multishot accept and provided buffers. Handle short writes and connection cleanup.
acceptance_criteria: IORING_OP_ACCEPT is used for async connection acceptance; no blocking accept() calls
Multishot accept (IORING_ACCEPT_MULTISHOT) is used where kernel supports it, reusing a single SQE for multiple accepts
Receive operations use provided buffer rings (IORING_OP_RECV with buffer group ID) to avoid pre-allocating per-connection buffers
Short writes (CQE res < requested send size) are handled by resubmitting the remaining bytes
Connection lifecycle (accept → read → process → write → close) is managed entirely through io_uring without epoll
Connection cleanup cancels all in-flight SQEs for a disconnected socket using IORING_OP_ASYNC_CANCEL before closing the fd
Server handles at least 10,000 concurrent connections with stable latency (p99 < 10ms for echo of 64-byte messages)
Provided buffer ring is replenished when buffer count drops below 25% of initial capacity
pitfalls: Multishot accept CQEs have IORING_CQE_F_MORE flag set; its absence means the multishot was terminated and must be resubmitted
Provided buffers for recv require checking CQE flags (IORING_CQE_F_BUFFER) and extracting buffer ID from cqe->flags >> IORING_CQE_BUFFER_SHIFT
Connection cleanup without ASYNC_CANCEL leaks SQEs and causes use-after-free when CQEs arrive for closed fds
Forgetting to replenish provided buffer rings under load causes ENOBUFS errors for new recv operations
TCP_NODELAY should be set on accepted sockets to avoid Nagle's algorithm adding latency to small writes
deliverables: TCP echo server with IORING_OP_ACCEPT for async connection handling
Multishot accept implementation with automatic resubmission on termination
Provided buffer ring integration for receive operations
Connection state tracker managing per-connection in-flight operations
Short write handler for partial send completions
Scalability test demonstrating 10K+ concurrent connections

milestones.name: Zero-copy, SQ Polling, Linked Ops & Benchmarks
milestones.description: Implement advanced io_uring features including zero-copy sends, SQ polling mode, linked SQE chains, and IO_DRAIN ordering. Produce comprehensive benchmarks comparing io_uring vs epoll under varied workloads.
concepts: CQE notification flag (IORING_CQE_F_NOTIF) for zero-copy buffer lifecycle
skills: SQ polling configuration and idle timeout tuning
acceptance_criteria: IORING_OP_SEND_ZC is used for network sends; buffer is not freed until the notification CQE (IORING_CQE_F_NOTIF flag) is received
Each zero-copy send produces two CQEs — the completion CQE and the notification CQE; both are correctly harvested and distinguished
SQ polling mode (IORING_SETUP_SQPOLL) is enabled with configurable idle timeout; benchmark shows reduced syscall count vs non-SQPOLL mode
Linked SQEs (IOSQE_IO_LINK) create operation chains where failure of any link cancels subsequent operations in the chain
IOSQE_IO_DRAIN is used where ordering is required; benchmark demonstrates the throughput cost of IO_DRAIN vs unordered submission
Comprehensive benchmark suite measures latency (p50, p95, p99) and throughput for io_uring vs epoll under at least 3 workloads — file I/O heavy, network I/O heavy, and mixed
Benchmark results include syscall counts (measured via strace or perf) showing io_uring's batching advantage
All advanced features gracefully fall back or report clear errors on unsupported kernel versions
pitfalls: Zero-copy send requires kernel 6.0+; IORING_OP_SEND_ZC returns EINVAL on older kernels
Zero-copy notification CQE can arrive out of order relative to other CQEs; buffer tracking must handle this
SQPOLL thread consumes CPU even when idle until sq_thread_idle timeout; set it appropriately (default 1000ms is often too high)
SQPOLL requires CAP_SYS_NICE or io_uring_register with IORING_REGISTER_IOWQ_AFF on unprivileged processes
Linked SQEs fail as a chain — if operation N fails, operations N+1..end are cancelled with -ECANCELED
IO_DRAIN forces serialization of all prior SQEs; using it frequently defeats the purpose of async I/O
deliverables: Zero-copy send implementation with dual-CQE handling (completion + notification)
SQ polling mode configuration with idle timeout tuning and privilege handling
Linked SQE chains for compound operations with correct error propagation
IO_DRAIN usage for operations requiring ordering guarantees
Benchmark suite with latency percentiles, throughput, and syscall count comparison across io_uring vs epoll

blueprint.title: io_uring High-Performance Server
blueprint.overview:
Build a high-performance TCP server using Linux io_uring, the modern asynchronous I/O interface that eliminates the syscall overhead inherent in traditional epoll-based event loops. Unlike epoll which requires a syscall per operation (epoll_wait + read/write), io_uring uses shared memory ring buffers between kernel and userspace, enabling batched submissions and completions with minimal kernel transitions. This project progresses from fundamental SQ/CQ ring management through advanced features including zero-copy I/O, provided buffer rings, SQ polling, and linked operations, culminating in comprehensive benchmarks against epoll.

The core insight is that io_uring inverts the traditional I/O model: instead of the application asking the kernel 'is data ready?' and then 'give me data,' the application posts requests to a queue and the kernel posts completions to another queue—all through shared memory that both sides can read without syscalls. This architectural shift enables throughput improvements of 2-10x for I/O-bound workloads while reducing CPU usage significantly.
blueprint.design_philosophy:
This project teaches the fundamental tension between software's desire for simple, synchronous abstractions and hardware's reality of latency, parallelism, and cache hierarchies. io_uring represents Linux's answer to a decades-old problem: every syscall costs 100-1000+ cycles for context switching, and traditional async I/O (epoll) requires multiple syscalls per operation. By mapping ring buffers into shared memory, io_uring lets applications submit dozens of I/O requests with a single syscall—or zero syscalls with SQ polling.

The progression mirrors the learner's growing understanding: first master the ring buffer mechanics and memory barriers (the 'physics' of the interface), then apply it to file I/O (simplest async pattern), extend to networking (state machines and connection lifecycle), and finally unlock advanced optimizations (zero-copy, polling, operation chains). Each milestone reveals a misconception about how I/O 'should' work versus how hardware actually operates.

implementation.rationale: io_uring is a Linux kernel interface with direct memory-mapped structures defined in kernel headers. C provides unmediated access to these structures, explicit memory barrier intrinsics (io_uring_smp_store_release/load_acquire), and direct syscall invocation via syscall(). The learning objectives—ring buffer memory layout, cache line awareness, memory barrier placement, and zero-copy buffer lifecycle—are all best understood through C's transparent memory model. Rust with nix crate or liburing bindings would add abstraction layers that obscure the very mechanics being taught.
implementation.style_guide: Linux kernel coding style (K&R braces, 8-space tabs, 80-column limit for documentation). Use liburing for setup convenience but implement core ring operations manually for educational clarity. All struct layouts must include byte offset comments. Memory barriers must be explicitly commented with their purpose. Error handling via negative errno return values matching kernel convention.
implementation.build_system: Makefile with separate targets for each milestone binary, benchmark suite, and test runner. CFLAGS must include -Wall -Wextra -O2 -D_GNU_SOURCE. Link against liburing (-luring) for setup helpers.
alternatives: Rust with io-uring crate — memory-safe but hides ring buffer mechanics behind abstractions
Zig — excellent for systems programming but smaller ecosystem and less kernel documentation alignment

assumed_known: C pointers, structs, and memory layout
File descriptors and basic POSIX I/O (open, read, write, close)
TCP sockets (socket, bind, listen, accept, connect)

must_teach_first.concept: Ring buffer index arithmetic (modulo-free circular queues)
Linux syscall ABI and errno conventions

milestones.title: Basic SQ/CQ Operations & Buffer Strategies
milestones.summary: Establish the foundational io_uring interface: initialize the ring via io_uring_setup, memory-map the shared submission and completion queues, implement correct memory barrier placement for producer/consumer synchronization, and master both fixed buffer registration and provided buffer rings for efficient buffer management.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think ring buffers are simple circular queues—write to tail, read from head, wrap around. They assume memory ordering 'just works' on x86 because it's a strong memory model. They expect buffer registration is an optimization detail, not a fundamental architectural choice with correctness implications.
milestones.reveal: io_uring rings are producer/consumer queues with split ownership: the application owns SQ tail and CQ head, the kernel owns SQ head and CQ tail. Each side only writes its owned index and reads the other's. Memory barriers are NOT optional—compiler reordering can cause the kernel to see stale tail values even on x86, leading to lost operations. Provided buffer rings fundamentally change buffer ownership: the kernel selects buffers, not the application, enabling true zero-allocation hot paths.
cascade: Lock-free programming — io_uring's split producer/consumer model is identical to MPSC queues in concurrent programming; understanding one unlocks the other
DMA and zero-copy (cross-domain) — fixed buffer registration pins user memory for device DMA; this is how high-performance storage (NVMe) and networking (DPDK) achieve zero-copy transfers
Cache coherence protocols — the memory barrier requirement reflects MESI protocol state transitions; acquire/load ensures invalidation visibility, release/store ensures writeback ordering
Batch processing systems — the 'submit N, reap M' pattern appears everywhere from GPU command buffers to database write-ahead logs
yaml_acceptance_criteria: io_uring instance is initialized with io_uring_setup syscall and SQ/CQ rings are mmap'd into userspace
SQ and CQ sizes are configured correctly — CQ is at least 2x SQ size (kernel default) to prevent CQ overflow
Memory barriers (io_uring_smp_store_release for tail, io_uring_smp_load_acquire for head) are correctly placed between ring updates
Batched submission demonstrated by submitting 10+ SQEs with a single io_uring_enter call and verifying all CQEs are harvested
Fixed buffers are registered with IORING_REGISTER_BUFFERS and used with IORING_OP_READ_FIXED/WRITE_FIXED; benchmark shows reduced per-op overhead vs non-fixed
Provided buffer ring is set up with IORING_REGISTER_PBUF_RING; operations select buffers from the ring and the buffer group ID is correctly specified in SQEs
CQE error handling checks res field for negative errno values and handles EAGAIN, ECANCELED, and EBUSY appropriately
All registered buffers and rings are properly unregistered during cleanup

milestones.title: File I/O Server with Registered Resources
milestones.summary: Build an asynchronous file server using io_uring for all I/O operations. Implement registered file descriptors to eliminate per-operation fd table lookups, handle short reads correctly by resubmitting partial operations, and implement Direct I/O with proper buffer alignment for maximum throughput.
milestones.misconception: Developers believe that a read() for N bytes either returns N bytes or an error. They think file descriptors are just integers that the kernel 'knows about.' They assume Direct I/O is just a flag that bypasses the page cache with no other requirements.
milestones.reveal: Short reads are not errors—they're normal async behavior. A read for 4096 bytes may return 3000 because that's all currently available (pipes, sockets) or because of EOF proximity. Registered file descriptors aren't just 'known to the kernel'—they bypass the entire fd table lookup path, reducing per-operation overhead by 10-50ns. Direct I/O requires 512-byte or 4K-byte aligned buffers AND aligned file offsets; misalignment causes EINVAL, not graceful fallback.
cascade: Database storage engines — the short-read handling and buffer pool patterns are identical to how LSM trees and B-trees manage page reads
Storage stack internals (cross-domain) — Direct I/O reveals the block layer's segment merging and I/O scheduler behavior; understanding this explains NVMe queue depths
POSIX async I/O history — io_uring's short read handling contrasts with POSIX AIO's signal-based completion, showing why kernel-managed rings won
Filesystem journaling — the buffer lifetime requirements (don't reuse until CQE) mirror write-ahead log buffer management in databases
yaml_acceptance_criteria: File server serves read requests using IORING_OP_READ with correct file descriptor and offset parameters
File descriptors are registered with IORING_REGISTER_FILES and SQEs use IOSQE_FIXED_FILE flag with the registered index
Short reads (CQE res < requested bytes) are handled by resubmitting a read for the remaining bytes at the updated offset
Multiple concurrent file reads (at least 64 in-flight) are handled with correct buffer-to-request association
Direct I/O (O_DIRECT) variant is implemented with properly aligned buffers (512-byte or 4K-byte alignment depending on filesystem)
Benchmark demonstrates at least 2x throughput improvement over synchronous pread for 64+ concurrent 4KB random reads on SSD
Buffer lifetime is guaranteed to extend until CQE is harvested; no buffer is reused while an async operation references it

milestones.title: TCP Network Server
milestones.summary: Construct a complete TCP echo server using io_uring for accept, receive, send, and close operations. Implement multishot accept to handle connection bursts with a single SQE, integrate provided buffer rings for receive operations to eliminate per-connection buffer pre-allocation, and handle short writes and connection cleanup with proper cancellation.
milestones.misconception: Developers think accepting connections requires one accept() call per connection. They assume TCP send() delivers all bytes or fails. They believe closing a socket immediately terminates all associated operations. They expect provided buffers work like regular buffers—just pass a pointer.
milestones.reveal: Multishot accept (IORING_ACCEPT_MULTISHOT) allows a single SQE to generate multiple CQEs—one per accepted connection—eliminating accept loop overhead entirely. TCP send() on non-blocking sockets returns the number of bytes accepted into the send buffer, which may be less than requested; the remaining bytes must be resubmitted. Closing a socket does NOT cancel in-flight async operations; their CQEs will still arrive, potentially causing use-after-free if buffers were reused. Provided buffers require extracting the buffer ID from CQE flags (IORING_CQE_F_BUFFER), not using a pre-selected pointer.
cascade: Game server architecture — the 10K+ concurrent connection pattern and per-connection state machines are foundational to MMO and multiplayer server design
HTTP/2 and QUIC (cross-domain) — the multishot pattern and provided buffer selection mirror how these protocols multiplex streams over single connections
Load balancer design — the connection lifecycle management and cancellation patterns apply directly to proxy servers and API gateways
Operating system schedulers — the provided buffer ring replenishment under load mirrors how kernel network stacks manage sk_buff pools
yaml_acceptance_criteria: IORING_OP_ACCEPT is used for async connection acceptance; no blocking accept() calls
Multishot accept (IORING_ACCEPT_MULTISHOT) is used where kernel supports it, reusing a single SQE for multiple accepts
Receive operations use provided buffer rings (IORING_OP_RECV with buffer group ID) to avoid pre-allocating per-connection buffers
Short writes (CQE res < requested send size) are handled by resubmitting the remaining bytes
Connection lifecycle (accept → read → process → write → close) is managed entirely through io_uring without epoll
Connection cleanup cancels all in-flight SQEs for a disconnected socket using IORING_OP_ASYNC_CANCEL before closing the fd
Server handles at least 10,000 concurrent connections with stable latency (p99 < 10ms for echo of 64-byte messages)
Provided buffer ring is replenished when buffer count drops below 25% of initial capacity

milestones.title: Zero-copy, SQ Polling, Linked Ops & Benchmarks
milestones.summary: Implement the most powerful io_uring features: zero-copy network sends that eliminate buffer copying to kernel space, SQ polling mode that removes syscall overhead entirely for submission-bound workloads, linked SQE chains for dependent operations with atomic failure semantics, and comprehensive benchmarks demonstrating io_uring's advantages over epoll.
milestones.misconception: Developers think 'zero-copy' means the kernel never touches the data—somehow DMA goes directly from user buffer to network card. They assume SQ polling is always faster because 'no syscalls.' They believe linked operations are just syntactic sugar for 'submit these in order.'
milestones.reveal: Zero-copy send (IORING_OP_SEND_ZC) means the kernel doesn't COPY data to its own buffers—it builds network packet headers in kernel space and DMAs from the user buffer directly. The buffer must remain valid until the NOTIFICATION CQE arrives (separate from the completion CQE), not just until the operation 'completes.' SQ polling burns a CPU core polling for submissions; it's faster only when syscall overhead dominates (many small operations) and hurts when I/O latency dominates. Linked SQEs have failure propagation: if operation 3 fails, operations 4-N are automatically cancelled with -ECANCELED, enabling atomic multi-step operations.
cascade: RDMA and InfiniBand (cross-domain) — zero-copy send mechanics mirror RDMA's memory registration and work request model, showing the convergence of high-performance networking APIs
Database transaction protocols — linked SQE failure propagation is identical to transaction rollback semantics; understanding one clarifies the other
Real-time systems — SQ polling's CPU-for-latency tradeoff appears in busy-wait vs interrupt-driven I/O debates across embedded and HFT systems
Microkernel design — the kernel/userspace shared memory model demonstrates how capability-based systems can eliminate context switches
yaml_acceptance_criteria: IORING_OP_SEND_ZC is used for network sends; buffer is not freed until the notification CQE (IORING_CQE_F_NOTIF flag) is received
Each zero-copy send produces two CQEs — the completion CQE and the notification CQE; both are correctly harvested and distinguished
SQ polling mode (IORING_SETUP_SQPOLL) is enabled with configurable idle timeout; benchmark shows reduced syscall count vs non-SQPOLL mode
Linked SQEs (IOSQE_IO_LINK) create operation chains where failure of any link cancels subsequent operations in the chain
IOSQE_IO_DRAIN is used where ordering is required; benchmark demonstrates the throughput cost of IO_DRAIN vs unordered submission
Comprehensive benchmark suite measures latency (p50, p95, p99) and throughput for io_uring vs epoll under at least 3 workloads — file I/O heavy, network I/O heavy, and mixed
Benchmark results include syscall counts (measured via strace or perf) showing io_uring's batching advantage
All advanced features gracefully fall back or report clear errors on unsupported kernel versions

diagrams.title: io_uring Server Architecture — Satellite Map
diagrams.description: System-wide view showing all milestones and their interconnections. Central hub is the io_uring ring buffer, with spokes to: M1 (Ring Foundations with buffer strategies), M2 (File I/O with registered resources), M3 (TCP Server with connection lifecycle), M4 (Advanced features with benchmarks). Each milestone shows its key data structures and kernel interfaces. Arrows indicate prerequisite relationships and shared components (buffer pools, SQE preparation helpers, CQE processing loop).

diagrams.title: SQ/CQ Ring Buffer Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Detailed memory layout showing the io_uring structure after io_uring_setup and mmap. Three regions: (1) SQ ring indices (head, tail, ring_mask, ring_entries, flags, dropped) on cache-line boundaries, (2) CQ ring indices plus CQE array, (3) SQE array. Byte offsets annotated. Arrows showing kernel reads SQ tail, writes SQ head and CQ tail; application writes SQ tail and CQ head, reads SQ head and CQ tail.

diagrams.title: Memory Barrier Placement — Before/After
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of incorrect vs correct memory barrier usage. Left: application updates SQ tail, kernel reads stale value due to store buffering, operation lost. Right: io_uring_smp_store_release on tail update ensures visibility before kernel can observe. Shows acquire/release as one-way barriers in the memory ordering model.

diagrams.title: Batched Submission — Submission Queue Flow
diagrams.description: Data walk showing: (1) application prepares SQEs at indices tail through tail+N-1, (2) application calls io_uring_enter once with count N, (3) kernel processes all N SQEs, (4) kernel posts N CQEs to completion queue, (5) application harvests all CQEs in single loop. Contrasted with N individual syscalls for traditional async I/O.

diagrams.title: Fixed Buffer Registration — Memory Pinning
diagrams.description: Structure layout showing iovec array passed to IORING_REGISTER_BUFFERS, kernel's internal page table entries marking pages as pinned (VM_PINNED), and how IORING_OP_READ_FIXED references buffers by index instead of pointer. Shows the performance path: no get_user_pages() call per operation.

diagrams.title: Provided Buffer Ring Architecture
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram showing provided buffer ring (struct io_uring_buf_ring) with head/tail indices, buffer ring entries (addr, len, bid). Flow: application posts buffers to ring, kernel selects buffer on recv operation, CQE contains buffer ID in flags field (IORING_CQE_F_BUFFER), application processes data and returns buffer to ring.

diagrams.title: Provided Buffer Selection — Trace Example
diagrams.description: Step-by-step trace: (1) application posts 16 buffers to ring, (2) IORING_OP_RECV submitted with buffer group ID, (3) kernel pops buffer from ring head, (4) data received directly into buffer, (5) CQE returned with buffer ID encoded, (6) application extracts buffer ID via cqe->flags >> IORING_CQE_BUFFER_SHIFT, (7) application replenishes ring after processing.

diagrams.title: CQE Error Code Decision Tree
diagrams.description: Decision tree for CQE res field interpretation: res >= 0 is byte count (success), res < 0 is -errno. Branches for EAGAIN (retry later), ECANCELED (operation cancelled, check if intentional), EBUSY (ring overflow, drain CQ), other errors (log and handle per-operation). Shows proper handling flow for each error class.

diagrams.title: Registered File Descriptor Table
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison: traditional fd table lookup (per-operation fdget() call, permission checks, reference counting) vs registered fd table (direct index into sparse array, no lookup overhead). Shows IORING_REGISTER_FILES creating the table and IOSQE_FIXED_FILE flag selecting the fast path.

diagrams.title: Short Read Handling — State Evolution
diagrams.description: State evolution diagram showing: Initial state (offset=0, remaining=4096), first read returns 3000, update state (offset=3000, remaining=1096), resubmit read for 1096 bytes at offset 3000, second read returns 1096, complete. Shows buffer slicing and offset calculation for partial completions.

diagrams.title: Direct I/O Alignment Requirements
diagrams.description: Structure layout showing required alignments: buffer address aligned to 512B or 4KB (depending on filesystem), buffer size aligned to block size, file offset aligned to block size. Shows misaligned scenario with EINVAL and properly aligned scenario with successful O_DIRECT read.

diagrams.title: Concurrent File I/O — 64 In-Flight Operations
diagrams.description: Data walk showing 64 concurrent reads in flight: 64 SQEs prepared with different file offsets and buffer pointers, single io_uring_enter submits all, kernel processes reads in parallel (block layer may merge adjacent), CQEs arrive in completion order (not submission order), application matches CQEs to requests via user_data field.

diagrams.title: Buffer Lifetime and Ownership Tracking
diagrams.description: State diagram showing buffer ownership: FREE → IN_USE (SQE prepared) → IN_FLIGHT (io_uring_enter called) → IN_USE (CQE received) → FREE (application done processing). Shows use-after-free scenario if buffer returned to pool before CQE harvested.

diagrams.title: File I/O Benchmark — io_uring vs pread
diagrams.description: Performance comparison chart: X-axis is concurrency level (1, 4, 16, 64 in-flight), Y-axis is throughput (MB/s). Three lines: synchronous pread, io_uring with regular buffers, io_uring with fixed buffers. Shows crossover point where batching advantage exceeds fixed overhead. Includes syscall count annotations.

diagrams.title: TCP Connection State Machine
diagrams.description: State evolution diagram for single connection: ACCEPTING → READING (IORING_OP_RECV posted) → PROCESSING (data in buffer, prepare response) → WRITING (IORING_OP_SEND posted) → CLOSING (IORING_OP_CLOSE posted). Shows transitions on CQE arrival, error handling branches, and in-flight operation tracking per state.

diagrams.title: Multishot Accept — Single SQE, Multiple CQEs
diagrams.description: Before/after comparison. Before: loop with individual accept SQEs, one per connection, resubmit on each CQE. After: single multishot accept SQE with IORING_ACCEPT_MULTISHOT, CQEs arrive with IORING_CQE_F_MORE flag indicating more coming, when flag absent the multishot terminated and must be resubmitted.

diagrams.title: Provided Buffer Ring for TCP Receive
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram showing TCP socket + provided buffer ring integration. Multiple connections share single buffer ring. IORING_OP_RECV submitted with IOSQE_BUFFER_SELECT and buf_group. Kernel selects buffer, receives data, returns CQE with buffer ID. Application tracks which buffer belongs to which connection.

diagrams.title: Short Write Handling — Partial Send Recovery
diagrams.description: Trace example: send(1000 bytes) returns 600, remaining 400 bytes must be resubmitted at buffer offset 600. Shows send buffer filling scenario on slow receiver, backpressure propagation, and eventual completion when receiver drains.

diagrams.title: Connection Cleanup with ASYNC_CANCEL
diagrams.description: Data walk showing proper cleanup: (1) connection error detected, (2) post IORING_OP_ASYNC_CANCEL with fd to cancel all in-flight ops, (3) wait for CQEs of cancelled ops (will have -ECANCELED), (4) free connection resources, (5) close fd. Contrasted with improper cleanup causing use-after-free.

diagrams.title: 10K Concurrent Connections — Scalability Test Architecture
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram showing test setup: multiple client machines generating load, server with io_uring instance (SQ size 1024, CQ size 4096), connection table (hash map from fd to state), provided buffer ring (2048 buffers), latency measurement points. Shows message flow for echo benchmark.

diagrams.title: Provided Buffer Ring Replenishment Under Load
diagrams.description: State evolution showing buffer ring depletion: initial 64 buffers, 50 connections arrive, ring drops to 14 buffers, threshold (25% = 16) triggers replenishment, application posts 48 new buffers. Shows ENOBUFS scenario if replenishment delayed.

diagrams.title: Zero-Copy Send — Kernel DMA from User Buffer
diagrams.description: Microscopic view showing: IORING_OP_SEND_ZC with user buffer pointer, kernel builds skb header in kernel memory, kernel programs DMA engine to read payload from user buffer, network card DMAs from user buffer to wire. Shows notification CQE lifecycle: completion CQE arrives first (send initiated), notification CQE arrives after DMA complete (buffer can be freed).

diagrams.title: Zero-Copy Dual CQE Sequence
diagrams.description: Timeline showing: T0 submit SEND_ZC, T1 completion CQE arrives (res=bytes_sent), T2 application MUST NOT free buffer, T3 notification CQE arrives (IORING_CQE_F_NOTIF set), T4 buffer can now be freed. Shows out-of-order scenario where notification CQE may arrive after subsequent operations' CQEs.

diagrams.title: SQ Polling Mode — Kernel Thread Architecture
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram: application posts SQEs to ring, SQPOLL kernel thread spins checking SQ tail for new entries, processes submissions without syscall. Shows idle timeout behavior (sq_thread_idle ms before thread sleeps), wakeup via IORING_ENTER with flags. Contrasted with non-SQPOLL requiring syscall per batch.

diagrams.title: SQ Polling CPU Tradeoff Analysis
diagrams.description: Before/after performance comparison. X-axis: operations per second. Y-axis: total CPU usage. Shows crossover point where SQPOLL saves more CPU from eliminated syscalls than it burns polling. Annotations for optimal sq_thread_idle values for different workloads.

diagrams.title: Linked SQE Chains — Atomic Multi-Step Operations
diagrams.description: Structure layout showing chain of SQEs linked by IOSQE_IO_LINK: (1) read file at offset X, (2) process data (custom opcode), (3) write result to socket. State evolution: all three execute in order, if step 2 fails, step 3 automatically cancelled with -ECANCELED. Shows failure propagation through chain.

diagrams.title: IO_DRAIN Ordering Guarantee
diagrams.description: Trace comparison: without DRAIN, SQEs 1,2,3 may complete in any order (CQE 3,1,2 valid). With DRAIN on SQE 3, SQEs 1,2 must complete before 3 begins. Shows serialization point and throughput impact: DRAIN forces sequential execution of all prior SQEs.

diagrams.title: Benchmark Suite — Three Workload Profiles
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram showing benchmark configurations: (1) File I/O heavy — 90% read/write, 10% network, (2) Network I/O heavy — 90% send/recv, 10% file, (3) Mixed — 50/50 split. For each: io_uring configuration, epoll configuration (for comparison), measurement points (latency percentiles, throughput, syscall count).

diagrams.title: io_uring vs epoll — Architectural Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side architecture comparison. epoll: (1) epoll_wait syscall, (2) for each ready fd: read syscall, process, write syscall. io_uring: (1) post read SQEs, (2) io_uring_enter (optional with SQPOLL), (3) harvest CQEs, (4) post write SQEs. Shows syscall count difference: epoll = O(events × 3), io_uring = O(batches).

diagrams.title: Benchmark Results — Latency and Throughput
diagrams.description: Performance charts: (1) Latency CDF for io_uring vs epoll under network-heavy load, (2) Throughput vs connection count, (3) Syscall count comparison. Shows io_uring advantages scaling with concurrency and batch size. Includes p50, p95, p99 annotations.

diagrams.title: Feature Availability and Fallback Matrix
diagrams.description: Table showing kernel version requirements: 5.1 (basic io_uring), 5.5 (provided buffer rings), 5.6 (registered files), 5.19 (IORING_REGISTER_PBUF_RING improvements), 6.0 (IORING_OP_SEND_ZC). For each feature: fallback behavior or clear error message on unsupported kernels.

tdd_blueprint.design_vision: Build a production-grade asynchronous I/O server using Linux io_uring, progressing from fundamental ring buffer operations through advanced zero-copy I/O, SQ polling, and linked operations. The architecture eliminates syscall overhead through batched submissions via shared memory ring buffers, enables scalable buffer management with provided buffer rings, and achieves maximum throughput with registered resources and kernel bypass techniques. The final system will demonstrate 2-10x performance improvement over traditional epoll-based servers across file I/O, network I/O, and mixed workloads.

modules.name: Basic SQ/CQ Operations & Buffer Strategies
modules.description: Establishes the foundational io_uring infrastructure: memory-mapped ring buffer setup, SQE/CQE management with correct memory barriers, fixed buffer registration for zero-copy, and provided buffer rings for scalable buffer selection. Does NOT handle file I/O semantics, connection management, or advanced features like SQPOLL.

specs.inputs: io_uring_params structure with queue depth configuration, buffer pool specifications (sizes, counts, alignment requirements), kernel feature flags
specs.outputs: Fully initialized io_uring instance with mmap'd SQ/CQ rings, registered buffer pools (fixed and provided), SQE preparation helpers, CQE harvesting loop
specs.abstractions: io_uring (encapsulates ring_fd, SQ/CQ pointers, masks), buffer_pool (fixed buffer array with registration state), provided_buffer_ring (kernel-managed buffer selection), sqe_builder (fluent SQE construction), cqe_iterator (safe CQE traversal with advance)
error_categories: EAGAIN - Ring full, cannot submit more SQEs
ECANCELED - Operation cancelled by kernel or application
EBUSY - Ring or resource temporarily unavailable
ENOBUFS - Provided buffer ring exhausted
EINVAL - Invalid parameters (alignment, size, flags)
ENOMEM - Memory allocation or mapping failed
ENOTSUP - Feature not supported on this kernel version
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded event loop with lock-free ring buffer access. Memory barriers (smp_store_release/smp_load_acquire) synchronize with kernel. No application-level locks needed for basic operations. Buffer pool requires tracking but not locking in single-threaded model.
performance_targets: SQE preparation: <50ns per operation (memory write only)
CQE harvest: <30ns per completion (memory read + advance)
Batched submission overhead: <100ns amortized per operation
Fixed buffer registration: one-time ~10-50μs for 64 buffers
Memory barrier cost: ~5-10ns on x86 (compiler barrier only)
CQ overflow detection: <10ns (check dropped counter)
Batch submission of 64+ SQEs with single io_uring_enter

diagrams.title: io_uring Server Architecture — Satellite Map
diagrams.description: High-level system overview showing io_uring ring buffers between application and kernel, with data flow paths for SQ submissions and CQ completions. Shows relationship to file descriptors, buffer pools, and kernel I/O subsystems.

diagrams.title: SQ/CQ Ring Buffer Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Detailed memory layout showing three mmap'd regions (SQ ring, CQ ring, SQE array), their offsets from io_uring_setup, field positions within each struct (head, tail, ring_mask, array, cqes), and cache line boundaries.

diagrams.title: io_uring_sqe Structure — 64-Byte Layout
diagrams.description: Byte-by-byte layout of SQE showing opcode, flags, ioprio, fd, off/addr2, addr, len, rw_flags union, user_data, buf_index/buf_group, personality, splice_fd_in, addr3, padding. Highlights cache-line alignment.

diagrams.title: io_uring_cqe Structure — 16-Byte Layout
diagrams.description: Byte layout of CQE showing user_data (8 bytes), res (4 bytes, signed), flags (4 bytes). Explains how res encodes both success (bytes) and error (-errno).

diagrams.title: Memory Barrier Placement — Before/After
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing correct vs incorrect barrier placement. Before: SQE write → barrier → tail update. After: tail load with acquire → barrier → CQE read. Shows race condition without barriers.

diagrams.title: Ring Index Arithmetic — Split Ownership Model
diagrams.description: State diagram showing SQ head/tail (kernel writes head, app writes tail) and CQ head/tail (app writes head, kernel writes tail). Index wrapping with ring_mask, power-of-2 optimization.

diagrams.title: Batched Submission Flow
diagrams.description: Step-by-step flow: get_sqe loop → prep_read for each → store_release(tail) → single io_uring_enter → harvest CQEs. Shows syscall count reduction from N to 1.

diagrams.title: Fixed Buffer Registration — Memory Pinning
diagrams.description: Shows iovec array → IORING_REGISTER_BUFFERS syscall → kernel pins pages → returns registered buffer indices. Subsequent READ_FIXED uses buf_index instead of addr.

diagrams.title: Provided Buffer Ring Architecture
diagrams.description: Structure of io_uring_buf_ring: header with tail pointer, followed by io_uring_buf entries (addr, len, bid). Shows kernel consuming from head, app replenishing at tail.

diagrams.title: Buffer Selection — Trace Example
diagrams.description: Trace of RECV with BUFFER_SELECT: SQE with buf_group → kernel selects buffer → CQE with IORING_CQE_F_BUFFER and bid in flags → app extracts buffer → processes → returns to ring.

diagrams.title: CQE Error Code Decision Tree
diagrams.description: Decision tree for handling cqe->res: if negative, extract errno, branch on EAGAIN/ECANCELED/EBUSY/ENOBUFS/EBADF/EINVAL. Shows retry logic for transient errors.

diagrams.title: Resource Cleanup Sequence
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram for teardown: unregister buffers → unregister files → unregister pbuf_ring → munmap SQ ring → munmap CQ ring → munmap SQEs → close ring_fd.

modules.name: File I/O Server with Registered Resources
modules.description: Implements asynchronous file I/O using registered file descriptors and fixed buffers. Handles the critical short-read problem with resubmission logic, implements Direct I/O with proper alignment, and manages buffer ownership to prevent use-after-free bugs. Does NOT handle network I/O, connection management, or zero-copy sends.

specs.inputs: File paths to serve, read offsets and lengths, Direct I/O flag, concurrency level, registered file table updates
specs.outputs: Asynchronous file reads with completion callbacks, registered file descriptor table, buffer pool with ownership tracking, benchmark results vs synchronous pread
specs.abstractions: file_table (registered fd → kernel index mapping), read_request (state machine for in-progress reads), buffer_pool (ownership-tracked buffers), short_read_handler (resubmission logic), dio_aligner (alignment validation and adjustment)
error_categories: EINVAL - Buffer/offset not aligned for O_DIRECT
EIO - Physical I/O error, bad sector
ENOMEM - Could not lock pages for Direct I/O
EAGAIN - Resource temporarily unavailable
EBADF - Invalid file descriptor or index
EOVERFLOW - Offset beyond file maximum
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded event loop managing 64+ concurrent file reads. Buffer pool tracks ownership via state machine (FREE → SUBMITTED → PROCESSING → FREE). No locks needed; all state transitions happen in event loop thread.
performance_targets: Registered file lookup: 0ns (direct pointer, no table walk)
Short read resubmission: <200ns (SQE prep + tail update)
64+ concurrent reads at 2x throughput vs synchronous pread
Direct I/O latency: within 5% of buffered I/O for aligned reads
Buffer pool acquire/release: <50ns each
EOF detection: immediate (res=0 in CQE)
Zero per-operation fd validation overhead with registered files

implementation_phases.name: Async File Read with Fixed Buffers

diagrams.title: Registered File Descriptor Table
diagrams.description: Shows mapping from application fd → registered index → kernel struct file pointer. Compares traditional fdget_pos() path (lookup + lock + refcount) with registered path (direct pointer dereference).

diagrams.title: File Descriptor Table Update Flow
diagrams.description: Sequence for IORING_REGISTER_FILES_UPDATE: prepare fds array with -1 for empty slots → syscall with offset → kernel updates internal table → return success/error per slot.

diagrams.title: Short Read Handling — State Evolution
diagrams.description: State machine for read_request: INIT → SUBMITTED → [SHORT_READ → RESUBMIT → SUBMITTED]* → COMPLETE or ERROR. Shows bytes_completed accumulating, offset advancing.

diagrams.title: Direct I/O Alignment Requirements
diagrams.description: Memory layout showing required alignments: buffer address % 4096 == 0, offset % block_size == 0, length % block_size == 0. Shows EINVAL results for misaligned requests.

diagrams.title: Buffer Lifetime State Machine
diagrams.description: States: FREE (available) → SUBMITTED (SQE references it) → PROCESSING (CQE received, data being handled) → FREE. Shows illegal transitions and use-after-free bug scenario.

diagrams.title: Buffer Ownership Tracking
diagrams.description: Tracked buffer struct with addr, size, state, owner_user_data. Shows acquire (find FREE, set SUBMITTED) and release (set FREE, clear owner) operations.

diagrams.title: Concurrent File I/O — 64 In-Flight Operations
diagrams.description: Architecture showing request slots array, buffer pool, and SQ/CQ interaction. Multiple SQEs submitted, CQEs arrive out-of-order, matched to requests via user_data.

diagrams.title: Async Read Request Structure
diagrams.description: Memory layout of async_read_op: user_data, file_index, start_offset, current_offset, total_requested, bytes_completed, buffer pointer, status enum, error_code.

diagrams.title: File I/O Benchmark Results
diagrams.description: Bar chart comparing pread (1 syscall/op) vs io_uring batched (1 syscall/64 ops) vs io_uring registered. Shows ops/sec, latency percentiles, syscall counts.

diagrams.title: Complete File Server Architecture
diagrams.description: Module architecture showing file_server containing io_uring, file_slot array, buffer_slot array, read_request array, statistics counters. Method relationships.

modules.name: TCP Network Server
modules.description: Builds a TCP echo server using io_uring for all socket operations: accept, recv, send, close. Implements multishot accept for efficient connection handling, uses provided buffer rings for receive operations, handles short writes correctly, and manages connection cleanup safely with operation cancellation. Targets 10,000+ concurrent connections.

specs.inputs: Listen port, connection timeout, buffer pool configuration, maximum concurrent connections
specs.outputs: TCP echo server accepting connections, echoing data back, handling 10K+ concurrent connections with stable latency (p99 < 10ms for 64-byte echo)
specs.abstractions: connection (fd, state, buffer refs, in-flight count), connection_table (fd → connection mapping with free list), echo_handler (recv → send state machine), safe_closer (cancel ops before close)
error_categories: ECONNRESET - Connection reset by peer
EPIPE - Broken pipe (peer closed)
ETIMEDOUT - Connection timeout
EAGAIN/EWOULDBLOCK - No data available (non-blocking)
EMFILE - Process file descriptor limit reached
ENFILE - System file descriptor limit reached
ENOBUFS - Provided buffer ring empty
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded event loop handling 10K+ connections. Connection table indexed by connection ID (not fd, to handle fd reuse safely). Per-connection state machine prevents races. Buffer pool shared across all connections via provided buffer ring.
performance_targets: Accept rate: 10,000+ connections/second with multishot accept
Echo latency p99: <10ms for 64-byte messages at 10K connections
Connection table lookup: O(1) via direct index
Buffer ring replenishment: <1μs per buffer
Safe connection close: <10μs including cancellation
Memory per connection: <1KB (no per-connection buffer pre-allocation)
Stable latency under load: p99 within 2x of p50

diagrams.title: TCP Connection State Machine
diagrams.description: States: FREE → ACTIVE → RECVING → SENDING → CANCELING → CLOSED. Transitions triggered by accept CQE, recv CQE, send CQE, cancel CQE, error conditions.

diagrams.title: Multishot Accept — Single SQE, Multiple CQEs
diagrams.description: Flow: submit one ACCEPT SQE → kernel generates CQE per connection with IORING_CQE_F_MORE flag → when flag absent, resubmit. Shows reduction from N SQEs to 1.

diagrams.title: Provided Buffer Ring for TCP Receive
diagrams.description: Architecture: buffer pool (256 buffers) → provided buffer ring (bgid) → RECV SQE with BUFFER_SELECT → kernel picks buffer → CQE with bid in flags → app processes → returns buffer.

diagrams.title: Buffer Selection Flow — Trace Example
diagrams.description: Step-by-step trace: RECV on fd 5 with bgid=1 → kernel selects buffer 17 → CQE: res=128, flags=0x110000 (BUFFER + bid=17) → app reads pool->buffers[17] → returns buffer 17.

diagrams.title: Short Write Handling — Partial Send Recovery
diagrams.description: State evolution: send(buf, 4096) → CQE res=1024 → short write detected → resubmit with buf+1024, len=3072 → CQE res=3072 → complete. Shows offset tracking.

diagrams.title: Connection Cleanup with ASYNC_CANCEL
diagrams.description: Sequence: detect close needed → check n_in_flight → if >0, submit ASYNC_CANCEL with fd → wait for cancel CQE → then close(fd). Prevents use-after-free on fd reuse.

diagrams.title: fd Reuse Race Condition
diagrams.description: Shows bug scenario: T0 submit recv fd=5 → T1 close fd=5 → T2 accept gets fd=5 → T3 recv CQE arrives for OLD connection → processes with NEW connection context → corruption.

diagrams.title: Connection Structure Layout
diagrams.description: Memory layout: fd (4), state (1), padding (3), id (4), recv_buffer_id (2), recv_len (8), send_total (8), send_done (8), n_in_flight (4), cancel_requested (1), padding (3), bytes_received (8), bytes_sent (8). Total: 64 bytes, cache-line aligned.

diagrams.title: Echo Server Event Loop
diagrams.description: Flow diagram: submit accept → wait CQE → dispatch by user_data type → handle_accept creates connection → submit recv → handle_recv submits send → handle_send submits next recv or returns buffer.

diagrams.title: 10K Connections Scalability Test
diagrams.description: Test architecture: load generator → 10K connections → echo server → measure latency distribution. Shows connection table sizing (16K entries), buffer pool sizing (2K buffers).

diagrams.title: User Data Encoding Scheme
diagrams.description: 64-bit user_data layout: bits 63-60 = operation type (ACCEPT/RECV/SEND/CANCEL), bits 59-48 = reserved, bits 47-0 = connection ID. Enables O(1) CQE dispatch.

diagrams.title: Buffer Ring Replenishment Under Load
diagrams.description: Monitoring flow: track available count → warn at 25% threshold → return buffers after send complete → io_uring_buf_ring_add + advance. Shows ENOBUFS prevention.

modules.name: Zero-copy, SQ Polling, Linked Ops & Benchmarks
modules.description: Implements advanced io_uring features: zero-copy sends with dual CQE handling, SQ polling mode to eliminate submission syscalls, linked SQE chains for atomic multi-step operations, and IO_DRAIN for ordering guarantees. Produces comprehensive benchmarks comparing io_uring vs epoll across file I/O, network I/O, and mixed workloads.

specs.inputs: Zero-copy enable flag, SQPOLL configuration (idle timeout, CPU affinity), workload profiles for benchmarking, comparison baseline (epoll)
specs.outputs: Zero-copy send implementation with notification CQE handling, SQPOLL mode with fallback, linked SQE chains with failure propagation, benchmark suite with latency percentiles and syscall counts

EPERM - SQPOLL requires CAP_SYS_NICE
ECANCELED - Operation cancelled due to chain failure
ENOENT - Nothing to cancel in ASYNC_CANCEL
ETIME - Benchmark timeout
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded event loop with optional SQPOLL kernel thread. Zero-copy sends require tracking buffer state across two CQEs. Linked SQEs are submitted atomically; failure propagates automatically. Benchmarks run sequentially to avoid interference.
performance_targets: Zero-copy send: 10-30% throughput improvement for large payloads (>4KB)
SQPOLL mode: 98%+ syscall reduction, sub-microsecond submission latency
Linked SQE overhead: <100ns per link (flag set only)
IO_DRAIN serialization cost: 2-8x slower than unordered (measure and document)
Benchmark accuracy: +/-5% variance across runs
io_uring vs epoll speedup: 2-4x throughput, 2-5x latency improvement at p99

diagrams.title: Zero-Copy Send — Kernel DMA from User Buffer
diagrams.description: Shows data path: user buffer (registered) → IORING_OP_SEND_ZC → kernel builds headers in small buffer → NIC DMA reads headers from kernel, payload from user buffer. No payload copy.

diagrams.title: Zero-Copy Buffer State Machine

tdd_accumulated_md:
# TDD

Build a production-grade asynchronous I/O server using Linux io_uring, progressing from fundamental ring buffer operations through advanced zero-copy I/O, SQ polling, and linked operations. The architecture eliminates syscall overhead through batched submissions via shared memory ring buffers, enables scalable buffer management with provided buffer rings, and achieves maximum throughput with registered resources and kernel bypass techniques. The final system will demonstrate 2-10x performance improvement over traditional epoll-based servers across file I/O, network I/O, and mixed workloads.



<!-- TDD_MOD_ID: io-uring-server-m1 -->
# Technical Design Document: Basic SQ/CQ Operations & Buffer Strategies
## Module Charter
This module establishes the foundational io_uring infrastructure for the high-performance server. It encapsulates the Linux io_uring system call interface, providing memory-mapped shared ring buffer management between userspace and kernel, submission queue entry (SQE) preparation with correct memory barrier semantics, completion queue entry (CQE) harvesting with overflow detection, and two buffer management strategies: fixed buffer registration for zero-copy I/O and provided buffer rings for kernel-managed buffer selection. This module does NOT handle file I/O semantics, network connection lifecycle, request routing, or advanced features like SQ polling, zero-copy sends, or linked operations. Upstream dependencies are the Linux kernel (5.1+ for basic io_uring, 5.19+ for provided buffer rings) and standard C library for memory allocation. Downstream consumers are the file I/O server and TCP network server modules. The critical invariant: all ring indices must be updated with appropriate memory barriers, and no buffer may be reused while referenced by an in-flight SQE.
---
## File Structure
```
io_uring_server/
├── include/
│   ├── io_uring_internal.h     # [1] Core structures, memory barriers, kernel definitions
│   ├── buffer_pool.h           # [2] Fixed and provided buffer ring abstractions
│   └── io_uring_wrapper.h      # [3] Public API for ring management
├── src/
│   ├── io_uring_setup.c        # [4] Ring initialization and mmap logic
│   ├── sqe_management.c        # [5] SQE preparation and submission helpers
│   ├── cqe_management.c        # [6] CQE harvesting and overflow detection
│   ├── buffer_registration.c   # [7] Fixed buffer registration implementation
│   └── provided_buffer_ring.c  # [8] Provided buffer ring setup and replenishment
└── tests/
    ├── test_ring_setup.c       # [9] Ring initialization tests
    ├── test_batch_submission.c # [10] Batched submission verification
    └── test_buffer_strategies.c# [11] Fixed and provided buffer tests
```
---
## Complete Data Model
### Core io_uring Structures
The kernel defines these structures in `<linux/io_uring.h>`. We replicate them here with explicit byte offsets for educational clarity and to avoid kernel header dependencies in some build environments.
#### struct io_uring_sqe — Submission Queue Entry (64 bytes)
| Offset | Size | Field | Description |
|--------|------|-------|-------------|
| 0x00 | 1 | opcode | Operation type (IORING_OP_READ, WRITE, etc.) |
| 0x01 | 1 | flags | SQE flags (IOSQE_FIXED_FILE, IO_LINK, etc.) |
| 0x02 | 2 | ioprio | I/O priority (0 = default) |
| 0x04 | 4 | fd | File descriptor or registered file index |
| 0x08 | 8 | off | File offset for read/write operations |
| 0x10 | 8 | addr | Buffer address (user pointer) |
| 0x18 | 4 | len | Buffer length or operation parameter |
| 0x1C | 4 | rw_flags | Operation-specific flags (union) |
| 0x20 | 8 | user_data | Request identifier (copied to CQE) |
| 0x28 | 2 | buf_index | Fixed buffer index for READ_FIXED/WRITE_FIXED |
| 0x2A | 2 | buf_group | Buffer group ID for provided buffer selection |
| 0x2C | 2 | personality | Credentials personality (0 = default) |
| 0x2E | 2 | splice_fd_in | Source fd for splice operations |
| 0x30 | 8 | addr3 | Third address for some operations |
| 0x38 | 8 | __pad2 | Padding to 64 bytes |
**Cache line alignment:** Each SQE is exactly 64 bytes (one cache line). Never straddles cache boundaries.
#### struct io_uring_cqe — Completion Queue Entry (16 bytes)
| Offset | Size | Field | Description |
|--------|------|-------|-------------|
| 0x00 | 8 | user_data | Copied from SQE, identifies the request |
| 0x08 | 4 | res | Result: bytes transferred or negative errno |
| 0x0C | 4 | flags | CQE flags (BUFFER, MORE, NOTIF for advanced ops) |
**Result interpretation:**
- `res > 0`: Success, value is bytes transferred
- `res == 0`: Success but no data (EOF on read)
- `res < 0`: Error, `-res` is errno value
#### struct io_uring_params — Ring Configuration (kernel output)
| Offset | Size | Field | Description |
|--------|------|-------|-------------|
| 0x00 | 4 | sq_entries | Number of SQ entries allocated |
| 0x04 | 4 | cq_entries | Number of CQ entries (usually 2x sq_entries) |
| 0x08 | 4 | flags | Feature flags (IORING_SETUP_*) |
| 0x0C | 4 | sq_thread_cpu | CPU for SQ polling thread |
| 0x10 | 4 | sq_thread_idle | SQ poll idle timeout (ms) |
| 0x14 | 4 | features | Kernel feature bitmap |
| 0x18 | 4 | wq_fd | Workqueue file descriptor |
| 0x1C | 12 | resv[3] | Reserved |
| 0x28 | 64 | sq_off | SQ ring offset structure |
| 0x68 | 64 | cq_off | CQ ring offset structure |
#### struct io_sqring_offsets — SQ Ring Offsets
| Offset | Size | Field | Description |
|--------|------|-------|-------------|
| 0x00 | 4 | head | Offset to head index (kernel writes) |
| 0x04 | 4 | tail | Offset to tail index (app writes) |
| 0x08 | 4 | ring_mask | Mask for index wrapping (entries - 1) |
| 0x0C | 4 | ring_entries | Number of ring entries |
| 0x10 | 4 | flags | Ring flags (IORING_SQ_*) |
| 0x14 | 4 | dropped | Count of dropped SQEs |
| 0x18 | 4 | array | Offset to SQE index array |
| 0x1C | 12 | resv[3] | Reserved |
### Internal Abstractions
```c
// io_uring_internal.h
#ifndef IO_URING_INTERNAL_H
#define IO_URING_INTERNAL_H
#include <stdint.h>
#include <stddef.h>
#include <stdbool.h>
#include <sys/types.h>
// Memory barrier definitions (x86 implementation)
// These compile to compiler barriers on x86 due to strong memory model
// On ARM, they would emit dmb instructions
static inline void io_uring_smp_store_release(volatile uint32_t *p, uint32_t v)
{
    __asm__ __volatile__("" ::: "memory");  // Compiler barrier
    *p = v;
}
static inline uint32_t io_uring_smp_load_acquire(volatile uint32_t *p)
{
    uint32_t v = *p;
    __asm__ __volatile__("" ::: "memory");  // Compiler barrier
    return v;
}
// Ring state tracking
typedef struct {
    int ring_fd;              // File descriptor from io_uring_setup
    // SQ state (application writes tail, reads head)
    uint32_t *sq_head;        // Kernel writes, app reads
    uint32_t *sq_tail;        // App writes, kernel reads
    uint32_t *sq_ring_mask;   // entries - 1, for fast modulo
    uint32_t *sq_ring_entries;
    uint32_t *sq_flags;
    uint32_t *sq_dropped;     // Overflow counter
    uint32_t *sq_array;       // Index array for SQE lookup
    struct io_uring_sqe *sqes; // SQE buffer base
    uint32_t sq_entries;      // Cached count
    // CQ state (kernel writes tail, app reads)
    uint32_t *cq_head;        // App writes, kernel reads
    uint32_t *cq_tail;        // Kernel writes, app reads
    uint32_t *cq_ring_mask;
    uint32_t *cq_ring_entries;
    uint32_t *cq_overflow;    // Overflow counter
    struct io_uring_cqe *cqes; // CQE buffer base
    uint32_t cq_entries;      // Cached count
    // Memory regions for cleanup
    void *sq_ring_mem;
    size_t sq_ring_size;
    void *cq_ring_mem;
    size_t cq_ring_size;
    void *sqes_mem;
    size_t sqes_size;
} io_uring_ring_t;
// Buffer pool for fixed buffer registration
typedef struct {
    struct iovec *iovecs;     // Buffer descriptors for registration
    void **buffers;           // Buffer pointers for access
    uint32_t *buffer_lens;    // Length of each buffer
    bool *in_use;             // Ownership tracking
    uint32_t n_buffers;       // Total buffer count
    uint32_t n_registered;    // Successfully registered count
    bool registered;          // Registration complete flag
} fixed_buffer_pool_t;
// Provided buffer ring (kernel 5.19+)
typedef struct {
    struct io_uring_buf *buf_ring;  // Ring structure in shared memory
    void **buffers;                 // Actual buffer pointers
    uint32_t *buffer_lens;
    uint16_t bgid;                  // Buffer group ID (0-65535)
    uint32_t ring_entries;          // Number of entries
    uint32_t buffer_size;           // Size of each buffer
    uint32_t available;             // Buffers currently in ring
    bool registered;                // Registration complete
} provided_buffer_ring_t;
// SQE builder for fluent construction
typedef struct {
    struct io_uring_sqe *sqe;
    io_uring_ring_t *ring;
    int error;                      // Non-zero if builder in error state
} sqe_builder_t;
// CQE iterator for safe traversal
typedef struct {
    io_uring_ring_t *ring;
    uint32_t start_head;
    uint32_t count;
    uint32_t current;
} cqe_iterator_t;
// Error codes (positive values for internal use, negative for kernel errno)
#define IOURING_OK              0
#define IOURING_ERR_NOMEM       1
#define IOURING_ERR_RING_FULL   2
#define IOURING_ERR_INVALID     3
#define IOURING_ERR_KERNEL      4
#define IOURING_ERR_NOTSUP      5
#define IOURING_ERR_OVERFLOW    6
// Kernel feature detection
typedef struct {
    bool provided_buffers;      // IORING_REGISTER_PBUF_RING (5.19+)
    bool send_zc;               // IORING_OP_SEND_ZC (6.0+)
    bool sqpoll;                // IORING_SETUP_SQPOLL (5.2+)
    int kernel_major;
    int kernel_minor;
} kernel_features_t;
#endif // IO_URING_INTERNAL_H
```

## Interface Contracts
### Ring Initialization
```c
/**
 * Initialize an io_uring instance with specified queue depths.
 * 
 * @param ring           Pointer to ring structure to initialize
 * @param sq_entries     Desired SQ depth (will be rounded up to power of 2)
 * @param cq_entries     Desired CQ depth (0 = use kernel default of 2x sq)
 * @param flags          Setup flags (IORING_SETUP_* or 0 for defaults)
 * @return               0 on success, IOURING_ERR_* on failure
 * 
 * Preconditions:
 *   - ring must point to valid memory
 *   - sq_entries must be > 0 and <= 32768
 *   - If cq_entries > 0, must be >= sq_entries
 * 
 * Postconditions on success:
 *   - ring->ring_fd is a valid file descriptor
 *   - All ring pointers are mapped and accessible
 *   - SQ and CQ are empty (head == tail)
 *   - Caller must call io_uring_cleanup() to release resources
 * 
 * Errors:
 *   IOURING_ERR_NOMEM    - mmap() failed
 *   IOURING_ERR_KERNEL   - io_uring_setup syscall failed (check errno)
 *   IOURING_ERR_INVALID  - Invalid parameters
 */
int io_uring_init(io_uring_ring_t *ring, 
                  uint32_t sq_entries,
                  uint32_t cq_entries,
                  uint32_t flags);
/**
 * Clean up an io_uring instance, releasing all resources.
 * 
 * @param ring           Pointer to initialized ring structure
 * 
 * Preconditions:
 *   - ring must have been successfully initialized
 *   - No in-flight operations (all CQEs harvested)
 * 
 * Postconditions:
 *   - All memory mappings released
 *   - Ring file descriptor closed
 *   - Ring structure zeroed (safe to reinitialize)
 */
void io_uring_cleanup(io_uring_ring_t *ring);
```
### SQE Management
```c
/**
 * Get the next available SQE slot.
 * 
 * @param ring           Initialized ring structure
 * @return               Pointer to SQE, or NULL if ring is full
 * 
 * The returned SQE is NOT yet committed to the ring. Caller must:
 *   1. Fill in SQE fields
 *   2. Call io_uring_submit() or io_uring_commit_sqe()
 * 
 * Thread safety: NOT thread-safe. Single-threaded use only.
 * 
 * Memory barrier: This function uses acquire semantics when reading
 * the head index to ensure we see kernel's latest consumption.
 */
struct io_uring_sqe *io_uring_get_sqe(io_uring_ring_t *ring);
/**
 * Commit a prepared SQE to the submission queue.
 * 
 * @param ring           Initialized ring structure
 * @param sqe            SQE previously obtained from io_uring_get_sqe()
 * 
 * This updates the SQ tail with release semantics, ensuring the kernel
 * sees all SQE field writes before seeing the tail update.
 * 
 * For batch submissions, prefer io_uring_submit_n() which commits
 * multiple SQEs with a single tail update.
 */
void io_uring_commit_sqe(io_uring_ring_t *ring, struct io_uring_sqe *sqe);
/**
 * Submit all pending SQEs to the kernel.
 * 
 * @param ring           Initialized ring structure
 * @return               Number of SQEs submitted, or negative error
 * 
 * This issues an io_uring_enter syscall to notify the kernel of
 * new submissions. With SQ polling enabled, this may return 0
 * immediately (kernel is already polling).
 * 
 * Errors (negative return):
 *   -EINTR   - Interrupted by signal (retry)
 *   -ENOMEM  - Kernel out of memory
 *   -EINVAL  - Invalid ring state
 */
int io_uring_submit(io_uring_ring_t *ring);
/**
 * Submit pending SQEs and wait for completions.
 * 
 * @param ring           Initialized ring structure
 * @param wait_nr        Minimum completions to wait for
 * @return               Number of SQEs submitted, or negative error
 * 
 * Blocks until at least wait_nr completions are available.
 * Set wait_nr = 0 for non-blocking submit.
 */
int io_uring_submit_and_wait(io_uring_ring_t *ring, uint32_t wait_nr);
/**
 * Prepare a read SQE (basic variant).
 * 
 * @param ring           Initialized ring structure
 * @param fd             File descriptor
 * @param buf            Destination buffer
 * @param len            Number of bytes to read
 * @param offset         File offset (-1 for current position)
 * @param user_data      Request identifier
 * @return               Pointer to prepared SQE, or NULL on error
 * 
 * The SQE is committed but NOT submitted. Call io_uring_submit()
 * after preparing all SQEs.
 */
struct io_uring_sqe *io_uring_prep_read(io_uring_ring_t *ring,
                                         int fd,
                                         void *buf,
                                         uint32_t len,
                                         int64_t offset,
                                         uint64_t user_data);
/**
 * Prepare a read SQE using fixed buffer.
 * 
 * @param ring           Initialized ring structure
 * @param fd             File descriptor (or registered file index with FIXED_FILE)
 * @param buf_index      Index into registered fixed buffer pool
 * @param len            Number of bytes to read (must fit in buffer)
 * @param offset         File offset
 * @param user_data      Request identifier
 * @return               Pointer to prepared SQE, or NULL on error
 * 
 * Requires: Fixed buffers must be registered via io_uring_register_buffers()
 */
struct io_uring_sqe *io_uring_prep_read_fixed(io_uring_ring_t *ring,
                                               int fd,
                                               uint32_t buf_index,
                                               uint32_t len,
                                               int64_t offset,
                                               uint64_t user_data);
```
### CQE Management
```c
/**
 * Peek at the next available CQE without blocking.
 * 
 * @param ring           Initialized ring structure
 * @param cqe_ptr        Output: pointer to CQE, or NULL if none available
 * @return               1 if CQE available, 0 if queue empty
 * 
 * The CQE is NOT removed from the queue. Call io_uring_cqe_seen()
 * after processing to advance the head.
 * 
 * Memory barrier: Uses acquire semantics when reading tail to ensure
 * we see CQE data before processing.
 */
int io_uring_peek_cqe(io_uring_ring_t *ring, struct io_uring_cqe **cqe_ptr);
/**
 * Wait for a CQE to become available.
 * 
 * @param ring           Initialized ring structure
 * @param cqe_ptr        Output: pointer to CQE
 * @return               0 on success, negative error on failure
 * 
 * Blocks until at least one completion is available.
 * 
 * Errors:
 *   -EINTR  - Interrupted by signal
 *   -ETIME  - Timeout (if timeout was set)
 */
int io_uring_wait_cqe(io_uring_ring_t *ring, struct io_uring_cqe **cqe_ptr);
/**
 * Mark a CQE as consumed, advancing the CQ head.
 * 
 * @param ring           Initialized ring structure
 * @param cqe            CQE that was processed
 * 
 * After this call, the CQE slot may be reused by the kernel.
 * The cqe pointer becomes invalid.
 */
void io_uring_cqe_seen(io_uring_ring_t *ring, struct io_uring_cqe *cqe);
/**
 * Advance CQ head by count entries (batch consumption).
 * 
 * @param ring           Initialized ring structure
 * @param count          Number of CQEs to consume
 * 
 * Use after processing multiple CQEs in a loop.
 * Does NOT check for overflow; caller must have verified.
 */
void io_uring_cq_advance(io_uring_ring_t *ring, uint32_t count);
/**
 * Check for CQ overflow.
 * 
 * @param ring           Initialized ring structure
 * @return               Number of dropped completions, 0 if none
 * 
 * If this returns non-zero, the ring is in an inconsistent state.
 * Some completions were lost. Recovery requires reinitialization.
 */
uint32_t io_uring_check_overflow(io_uring_ring_t *ring);
/**
 * Create an iterator for processing all available CQEs.
 * 
 * @param ring           Initialized ring structure
 * @return               Iterator structure for use with io_uring_cqe_next()
 */
cqe_iterator_t io_uring_cqe_iterator(io_uring_ring_t *ring);
/**
 * Get next CQE from iterator.
 * 
 * @param iter           Iterator from io_uring_cqe_iterator()
 * @param cqe_ptr        Output: pointer to CQE, or NULL if done
 * @return               true if CQE available, false if iteration complete
 */
bool io_uring_cqe_next(cqe_iterator_t *iter, struct io_uring_cqe **cqe_ptr);
/**
 * Complete iteration and advance CQ head.
 * 
 * @param iter           Iterator to complete
 * 
 * Must be called to advance the head after processing all CQEs.
 */
void io_uring_cqe_iterator_complete(cqe_iterator_t *iter);
```

## Algorithm Specification
### Algorithm: Ring Initialization with mmap
**Purpose:** Create a shared memory region between application and kernel containing the submission and completion queues.
**Inputs:**
- `sq_entries`: Desired SQ depth
- `cq_entries`: Desired CQ depth (0 for default)
- `flags`: Setup flags
**Outputs:**
- Fully mapped `io_uring_ring_t` structure
- Return code: 0 on success, error code on failure
**Procedure:**
1. **Validate parameters:**
   ```
   if sq_entries == 0 OR sq_entries > 32768:
       return IOURING_ERR_INVALID
   if cq_entries > 0 AND cq_entries < sq_entries:
       return IOURING_ERR_INVALID
   ```
2. **Issue io_uring_setup syscall:**
   ```
   params = zero-initialized io_uring_params
   params.flags = flags
   if cq_entries > 0:
       params.flags |= IORING_SETUP_CQSIZE
       params.cq_entries = cq_entries
   ring_fd = syscall(__NR_io_uring_setup, sq_entries, &params)
   if ring_fd < 0:
       return IOURING_ERR_KERNEL with errno
   ```
3. **Calculate mmap sizes:**
   ```
   sq_ring_size = params.sq_off.array + params.sq_entries * sizeof(uint32_t)
   cq_ring_size = params.cq_off.cqes + params.cq_entries * sizeof(io_uring_cqe)
   sqes_size = params.sq_entries * sizeof(io_uring_sqe)
   ```
4. **Map SQ ring:**
   ```
   sq_ring = mmap(NULL, sq_ring_size, PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE,
                  MAP_SHARED|MAP_POPULATE, ring_fd, IORING_OFF_SQ_RING)
   if sq_ring == MAP_FAILED:
       close(ring_fd)
       return IOURING_ERR_NOMEM
   ```
5. **Map SQE array (separate offset):**
   ```
   sqes = mmap(NULL, sqes_size, PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE,
               MAP_SHARED|MAP_POPULATE, ring_fd, IORING_OFF_SQES)
   if sqes == MAP_FAILED:
       munmap(sq_ring, sq_ring_size)
       close(ring_fd)
       return IOURING_ERR_NOMEM
   ```
6. **Map CQ ring:**
   ```
   cq_ring = mmap(NULL, cq_ring_size, PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE,
                  MAP_SHARED|MAP_POPULATE, ring_fd, IORING_OFF_CQ_RING)
   if cq_ring == MAP_FAILED:
       munmap(sqes, sqes_size)
       munmap(sq_ring, sq_ring_size)
       close(ring_fd)
       return IOURING_ERR_NOMEM
   ```
7. **Initialize ring pointers using offsets:**
   ```
   ring->sq_head = (uint32_t*)((char*)sq_ring + params.sq_off.head)
   ring->sq_tail = (uint32_t*)((char*)sq_ring + params.sq_off.tail)
   ring->sq_ring_mask = (uint32_t*)((char*)sq_ring + params.sq_off.ring_mask)
   ring->sq_array = (uint32_t*)((char*)sq_ring + params.sq_off.array)
   ring->sqes = sqes
   ring->sq_entries = params.sq_entries
   ring->cq_head = (uint32_t*)((char*)cq_ring + params.cq_off.head)
   ring->cq_tail = (uint32_t*)((char*)cq_ring + params.cq_off.tail)
   ring->cq_ring_mask = (uint32_t*)((char*)cq_ring + params.cq_off.ring_mask)
   ring->cqes = (io_uring_cqe*)((char*)cq_ring + params.cq_off.cqes)
   ring->cq_entries = params.cq_entries
   ```
8. **Verify initial state:**
   ```
   assert(*ring->sq_head == 0)
   assert(*ring->sq_tail == 0)
   assert(*ring->cq_head == 0)
   assert(*ring->cq_tail == 0)
   ```
**Invariants after execution:**
- `*sq_head <= *sq_tail` (SQ empty initially)
- `*cq_head == *cq_tail` (CQ empty initially)
- All pointers are valid mapped memory
- Ring is ready for SQE submission
